title: Video game

infobox.value: is an [electronic game](/wiki/Electronic_game "Electronic game") that involves interaction with a [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") or [input device](/wiki/Input_device "Input device") (such as a [joystick](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick"), [controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller"), [keyboard](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard"), or [motion sensing](/wiki/Motion_sensing "Motion sensing") device) to generate [visual](/wiki/Visual "Visual") [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") from a [display device](/wiki/Display_device "Display device"), most commonly shown in a [video](/wiki/Video "Video") format on a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set"), [computer monitor](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), [flat-panel display](/wiki/Flat-panel_display "Flat-panel display") or [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") on [handheld devices](/wiki/Handheld_device "Handheld device"), or a [virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset"). Most modern video games are [audiovisual](/wiki/Audiovisual "Audiovisual"), with [audio](/wiki/Sound "Sound") complement delivered through [speakers](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") or [headphones](/wiki/Headphones "Headphones"), and sometimes also with other types of sensory feedback (e.g., [haptic technology](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology") that provides [tactile](/wiki/Touch "Touch") sensations). Some video games also allow [microphone](/wiki/Microphone "Microphone") and [webcam](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") inputs for [in-game chatting](/wiki/Voice_chat_in_online_gaming "Voice chat in online gaming") and [livestreaming](/wiki/Video_game_livestreaming "Video game livestreaming").

A **video game**,[[a]](#cite_note-1) **computer game**,[[b]](#cite_note-2) or simply **game** is an [electronic game](/wiki/Electronic_game "Electronic game") that involves interaction with a [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") or [input device](/wiki/Input_device "Input device") (such as a [joystick](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick"), [controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller"), [keyboard](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard"), or [motion sensing](/wiki/Motion_sensing "Motion sensing") device) to generate [visual](/wiki/Visual "Visual") [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") from a [display device](/wiki/Display_device "Display device"), most commonly shown in a [video](/wiki/Video "Video") format on a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set"), [computer monitor](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), [flat-panel display](/wiki/Flat-panel_display "Flat-panel display") or [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") on [handheld devices](/wiki/Handheld_device "Handheld device"), or a [virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset"). Most modern video games are [audiovisual](/wiki/Audiovisual "Audiovisual"), with [audio](/wiki/Sound "Sound") complement delivered through [speakers](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") or [headphones](/wiki/Headphones "Headphones"), and sometimes also with other types of sensory feedback (e.g., [haptic technology](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology") that provides [tactile](/wiki/Touch "Touch") sensations). Some video games also allow [microphone](/wiki/Microphone "Microphone") and [webcam](/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") inputs for [in-game chatting](/wiki/Voice_chat_in_online_gaming "Voice chat in online gaming") and [livestreaming](/wiki/Video_game_livestreaming "Video game livestreaming").

Video games are typically categorized according to their [hardware platform](/wiki/Hardware_platform "Hardware platform"), which traditionally includes [arcade video games](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game"), [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game"), and [computer games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") (which includes [LAN games](/wiki/Network_game "Network game"), [online games](/wiki/Online_game "Online game"), and [browser games](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game")). More recently, the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry") has expanded onto [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming") through [mobile devices](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device") (such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphones "Smartphones") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computers "Tablet computers")), [virtual](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") and [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") systems, and remote [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming"). Video games are also classified into a wide range of [genres](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre") based on their style of [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay") and [target audience](/wiki/Target_audience "Target audience").

The first video game prototypes in the 1950s and 1960s were simple extensions of electronic games using video-like output from large, room-sized [mainframe computers](/wiki/Mainframe_computers "Mainframe computers"). The first consumer video game was the arcade video game *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")* in 1971, which took inspiration from the earlier 1962 computer game *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")*. In 1972 came the now-iconic video game *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* and the first [home console](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console"), the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"). The industry grew quickly during the ["golden age" of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") from the late 1970s to early 1980s but suffered from the [crash of the North American video game market in 1983](/wiki/1983_video_game_crash "1983 video game crash") due to loss of publishing control and saturation of the market. Following the crash, the industry matured, was dominated by Japanese companies such as [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"), and [Sony](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment"), and established practices and methods around the development and distribution of video games to prevent a similar crash in the future, many of which continue to be followed. In the 2000s, the core industry centered on "[AAA](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)")" games, leaving little room for riskier experimental games. Coupled with the availability of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") and [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution_of_video_games "Digital distribution of video games"), this gave room for [independent video game development](/wiki/Independent_video_game_development "Independent video game development") (or "[indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game")") to gain prominence into the 2010s. Since then, the commercial importance of the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry") has been increasing. The emerging Asian markets and proliferation of smartphone games in particular are altering player demographics towards [casual](/wiki/Casual_games "Casual games") and [cozy gaming](/wiki/Cozy_game "Cozy game"), and increasing [monetization](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization") by incorporating [games as a service](/wiki/Games_as_a_service "Games as a service").

Today, [video game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development") requires numerous skills, [vision](/wiki/Foresight_(psychology) "Foresight (psychology)"), [teamwork](/wiki/Teamwork "Teamwork"), and [liaisons](/wiki/Collaboration "Collaboration") between different parties, including [developers](/wiki/Video_game_developers "Video game developers"), [publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publishers "Video game publishers"), [distributors](/wiki/Distribution_(marketing) "Distribution (marketing)"), [retailers](/wiki/Retailer "Retailer"), [hardware](/wiki/Electronic_hardware "Electronic hardware") manufacturers, and other marketers, to successfully bring a game to its consumers. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game&action=edit), the global video game market had estimated annual revenues of US$159 billion across hardware, software, and services, which is three times the size of the global [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry") and four times that of the [film industry](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry") in 2019,[[1]](#cite_note-vg_industry_size_2020-3) making it a formidable heavyweight across the modern [entertainment industry](/wiki/Entertainment_industry "Entertainment industry"). The video game market is also a major influence behind the [electronics industry](/wiki/Electronics_industry "Electronics industry"), where [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") component, console, and peripheral sales, as well as consumer demands for better game performance, have been powerful driving factors for hardware design and innovation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Spacewar%21-PDP-1-20070512.jpg/250px-Spacewar%21-PDP-1-20070512.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spacewar!-PDP-1-20070512.jpg)

*[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962), an early [mainframe computer](/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer") game, pictured running on a [PDP-1](/wiki/PDP-1 "PDP-1") computer

Early video games used interactive electronic devices with various display formats. The earliest example dates to 1947—a "[cathode-ray tube amusement device](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube_amusement_device "Cathode-ray tube amusement device")" was filed for a patent on 25 January 1947, by [Thomas T. Goldsmith Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_T._Goldsmith_Jr. "Thomas T. Goldsmith Jr.") and Estle Ray Mann, and issued on 14 December 1948, as U.S. Patent 2455992.[[2]](#cite_note-4) Inspired by [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") display technology, it consisted of an analog device allowing a user to control the parabolic arc of a dot on the screen to simulate a missile being fired at targets, which were paper drawings fixed to the screen.[[3]](#cite_note-PongStory-5) Other early examples include [Christopher Strachey](/wiki/Christopher_Strachey "Christopher Strachey")'s *[Checkers](/wiki/Checkers_(video_game) "Checkers (video game)")*, the [Nimrod](/wiki/Nimrod_(computer) "Nimrod (computer)") computer at the 1951 [Festival of Britain](/wiki/Festival_of_Britain "Festival of Britain"); *[OXO](/wiki/OXO_(video_game) "OXO (video game)")*, a [tic-tac-toe](/wiki/Tic-tac-toe "Tic-tac-toe") computer game by [Alexander S. Douglas](/wiki/A.S._Douglas "A.S. Douglas") for the [EDSAC](/wiki/EDSAC "EDSAC") in 1952; *[Tennis for Two](/wiki/Tennis_for_Two "Tennis for Two")*, an electronic interactive game engineered by [William Higinbotham](/wiki/William_Higinbotham "William Higinbotham") in 1958; and *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")*, written by [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/MIT "MIT") students Martin Graetz, [Steve Russell](/wiki/Steve_Russell_(computer_scientist) "Steve Russell (computer scientist)"), and Wayne Wiitanen's on a DEC [PDP-1](/wiki/PDP-1 "PDP-1") computer in 1962. Each game had different means of display: NIMROD had a panel of lights to play the game of [Nim](/wiki/Nim "Nim"),[[4]](#cite_note-6) *OXO* had a graphical display to play tic-tac-toe,[[5]](#cite_note-7) *Tennis for Two* had an oscilloscope to display a side view of a tennis court,[[3]](#cite_note-PongStory-5) and *Spacewar!* had the [DEC](/wiki/Digital_Equipment_Corporation "Digital Equipment Corporation") PDP-1's vector display to have two [spaceships](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") battle each other.[[6]](#cite_note-8)

These inventions laid the foundation for modern video games. In 1966, while working at [Sanders Associates](/wiki/Sanders_Associates "Sanders Associates"), [Ralph H. Baer](/wiki/Ralph_H._Baer "Ralph H. Baer") devised a system to play a basic table tennis game on a television screen. With the company's approval, Baer created the prototype known as the "Brown Box". Sanders patented Baer's innovations and licensed them to [Magnavox](/wiki/Magnavox "Magnavox"), which commercialized the technology as the first home [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"), released in 1972.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKent200125-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) Separately, [Nolan Bushnell](/wiki/Nolan_Bushnell "Nolan Bushnell") and [Ted Dabney](/wiki/Ted_Dabney "Ted Dabney"), inspired by seeing *Spacewar!* running at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), devised a similar version running in a smaller coin-operated [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet") using a less expensive computer. This was released as *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")*, the first [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game"), in 1971.[[9]](#cite_note-11) Bushnell and Dabney went on to form [Atari, Inc.](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc."), and with [Allan Alcorn](/wiki/Allan_Alcorn "Allan Alcorn"), created their second [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") in 1972, the hit [ping pong](/wiki/Ping_pong "Ping pong")-style *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, which was directly inspired by the table tennis game on the Odyssey. Atari made a home version of *Pong*, which was released by Christmas 1975.[[3]](#cite_note-PongStory-5) The success of the Odyssey and *Pong*, both as an arcade game and home machine, launched the video game industry.[[10]](#cite_note-PBS-GameRev-Time-12)[[11]](#cite_note-InformIT-13) Both Baer and Bushnell have been titled "Father of Video Games" for their contributions.[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15)

## Terminology

The term "video game" was developed to describe [electronic games](/wiki/Electronic_game "Electronic game") played on a [video display](/wiki/Display_device "Display device") rather than on a [teletype printer](/wiki/Teletype_printer "Teletype printer"), [audio speaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker"), or similar device.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf20073–7-16) This also distinguished from [handheld electronic games](/wiki/Handheld_electronic_game "Handheld electronic game") such as *[Merlin](/wiki/Merlin_(console) "Merlin (console)"),* which commonly used LED lights for indicators not in combination for imaging purposes.[[15]](#cite_note-wolf_medium_chp1-17)

"Computer game" may also be used as a descriptor, as all these types of games essentially require the use of a computer processor; in some cases, it is used interchangeably with "video game".[[16]](#cite_note-vg_theory_reader_intro-18) Particularly in the United Kingdom and Western Europe, this is common due to the historic relevance of domestically produced microcomputers. Other terms used include digital game, for example, by the [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics").[[17]](#cite_note-19) The term "computer game" can also refer to [PC games](/wiki/PC_games "PC games"), which are played primarily on [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") or other flexible hardware systems, to distinguish them from [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game"), [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"), or [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game").[[15]](#cite_note-wolf_medium_chp1-17)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf20073–7-16)

Other terms, such as "television game", "telegame", or "TV game", had been used in the 1970s and early 1980s, particularly for [home gaming consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") that rely on connection to a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set").[[18]](#cite_note-20) However, these terms were also used interchangeably with "video game" in the 1970s, primarily due to "video" and "television" being synonymous.[[19]](#cite_note-21) In Japan, where consoles like the Odyssey were first imported and then made within the country by the large television manufacturers such as [Toshiba](/wiki/Toshiba "Toshiba") and [Sharp Corporation](/wiki/Sharp_Corporation "Sharp Corporation"), such games are known as "TV games", "TV geemu", or "terebi geemu".[[20]](#cite_note-geemu-22) The term "TV game" is still commonly used into the 21st century.[[20]](#cite_note-geemu-22)[[21]](#cite_note-23) "Electronic game" may also be used to refer to video games, but this also incorporates devices like early handheld electronic games that lack any video output.[[16]](#cite_note-vg_theory_reader_intro-18)

The first appearance of the term "video game" emerged around 1973. The [Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary") cited a 10 November 1973 *[BusinessWeek](/wiki/BusinessWeek "BusinessWeek")* article as the first printed use of the term.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Though Bushnell believed the term came from a vending magazine review of *Computer Space* in 1971,[[23]](#cite_note-25) a review of the major vending magazines *[Vending Times](/wiki/Vending_Times "Vending Times")* and *[Cashbox](/wiki/Cashbox_(magazine) "Cashbox (magazine)")* showed that the term may have come even earlier, appearing first in a letter dated July 10, 1972. In the letter, Bushnell uses the term "video game" twice.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Per video game historian Keith Smith, the sudden appearance suggested that the term had been proposed and readily adopted by those in the field. Around March 1973, Ed Adlum, who ran *Cashbox*'s coin-operated section until 1972 and then later founded *RePlay Magazine*, covering the coin-op amusement field, in 1975, used the term in an article in March 1973. In a September 1982 issue of *RePlay*, Adlum is credited with first naming these games as "video games": "RePlay's Eddie Adlum worked at 'Cash Box' when 'TV games' first came out. The personalities in those days were Bushnell, his sales manager Pat Karns, and a handful of other 'TV game' manufacturers like Henry Leyser and the McEwan brothers. It seemed awkward to call their products 'TV games', so borrowing a word from *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*'s description of movie jukeboxes, Adlum started to refer to this new breed of amusement machine as 'video games.' The phrase stuck."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Adlum explained in 1985 that up until the early 1970s, [amusement arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcades "Amusement arcades") typically had non-video [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_games "Arcade games") such as [pinball](/wiki/Pinball "Pinball") machines and [electro-mechanical games](/wiki/Electro-mechanical_game "Electro-mechanical game"). With the arrival of video games in arcades during the early 1970s, there was initially some confusion in the arcade industry over what term should be used to describe the new games. He "wrestled with descriptions of this type of game," alternating between "TV game" and "television game" but "finally woke up one day" and said, "What the hell... video game!"[[25]](#cite_note-27)

### Definition

While many games readily fall into a clear, well-understood definition of video games, new genres and innovations in game development have raised the question of what are the essential factors of a video game that separate the medium from other forms of entertainment.

The introduction of [interactive films](/wiki/Interactive_film "Interactive film") in the 1980s with games like *[Dragon's Lair](/wiki/Dragon%27s_Lair "Dragon's Lair")*, featured games with [full motion video](/wiki/Full_motion_video "Full motion video") played off a form of media but only limited user interaction.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerron2013259–280-28) This had required a means to distinguish these games from more traditional board games that happen to also use external media, such as the *Clue VCR Mystery Game* which required players to watch VCR clips between turns. To distinguish between these two, video games are considered to require some interactivity that affects the visual display.[[15]](#cite_note-wolf_medium_chp1-17)

Most video games tend to feature some type of victory or winning conditions, such as a scoring mechanism or a final [boss](/wiki/Boss_(video_game) "Boss (video game)") fight. The introduction of [walking simulators](/wiki/Walking_simulator "Walking simulator") ([adventure games](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game") that allow for exploration but lack any objectives) like *[Gone Home](/wiki/Gone_Home "Gone Home")*, and [empathy games](/wiki/Empathy_game "Empathy game") (video games that tend to focus on emotion) like *[That Dragon, Cancer](/wiki/That_Dragon,_Cancer "That Dragon, Cancer")* brought the idea of games that did not have any such type of winning condition and raising the question of whether these were actually games.[[27]](#cite_note-29) These are still commonly justified as video games as they provide a game world that the player can interact with by some means.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

The lack of any industry definition for a video game by 2021 was an issue during the case *[Epic Games v. Apple](/wiki/Epic_Games_v._Apple "Epic Games v. Apple")* which dealt with video games offered on Apple's [iOS App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)"). Among concerns raised were games like *[Fortnite Creative](/wiki/Fortnite_Creative "Fortnite Creative")* and *[Roblox](/wiki/Roblox "Roblox")* which created [metaverses](/wiki/Metaverse "Metaverse") of interactive experiences, and whether the larger game and the individual experiences themselves were games or not in relation to fees that Apple charged for the App Store. Judge [Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers](/wiki/Yvonne_Gonzalez_Rogers "Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers"), recognizing that there was yet an industry standard definition for a video game, established for her ruling that "At a bare minimum, video games appear to require some level of interactivity or involvement between the player and the medium" compared to passive entertainment like film, music, and television, and "videogames are also generally graphically rendered or animated, as opposed to being recorded live or via motion capture as in films or television".[[29]](#cite_note-pcgamer_epic_case_def-31) Rogers still concluded that what is a video game "appears highly eclectic and diverse".[[29]](#cite_note-pcgamer_epic_case_def-31)

### Video game terminology

See also: [Glossary of video game terms](/wiki/Glossary_of_video_game_terms "Glossary of video game terms")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Freedoom002_01.png/250px-Freedoom002_01.png)](/wiki/File:Freedoom002_01.png)

*[Freedoom](/wiki/Freedoom "Freedoom")*, a [clone](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") of the [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")*. Common elements include a [heads-up display](/wiki/HUD_(video_games) "HUD (video games)") along the bottom that includes the player's remaining [health](/wiki/Health_(game_terminology) "Health (game terminology)") and ammunition.

The gameplay experience varies radically between video games, but many common elements exist. Most games will launch into a [title screen](/wiki/Title_screen "Title screen") and give the player a chance to review options such as the number of players before starting a game. Most games are divided into [levels](/wiki/Level_(video_games) "Level (video games)") which the player must work the avatar through, [scoring points](/wiki/Score_(game) "Score (game)"), collecting [power-ups](/wiki/Power-up "Power-up") to boost the avatar's innate attributes, all while either using special attacks to defeat enemies or moves to avoid them. This information is relayed to the player through a type of on-screen [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") such as a [heads-up display](/wiki/HUD_(video_games) "HUD (video games)") atop the rendering of the game itself. Taking damage will deplete their avatar's [health](/wiki/Health_(game_terminology) "Health (game terminology)"), and if that falls to zero or if the avatar otherwise falls into an impossible-to-escape location, the player will lose one of their [lives](/wiki/Life_(video_games) "Life (video games)"). Should they lose all their lives without gaining an [extra life or "1-UP"](/wiki/Extra_life "Extra life"), then the player will reach the "[game over](/wiki/Game_over "Game over")" screen. Many levels as well as the game's finale end with a type of [boss](/wiki/Boss_(video_games) "Boss (video games)") character the player must defeat to continue on. In some games, intermediate points between levels will offer [save points](/wiki/Save_point "Save point") where the player can create a [saved game](/wiki/Saved_game "Saved game") on storage media to restart the game should they lose all their lives or need to stop the game and restart at a later time. These also may be in the form of a passage that can be written down and reentered at the title screen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Product flaws include [software bugs](/wiki/Software_bug "Software bug") which can manifest as [glitches](/wiki/Glitch "Glitch") which may be [exploited](/wiki/Exploit_(online_gaming) "Exploit (online gaming)") by the player; this is often the foundation of [speedrunning](/wiki/Speedrun "Speedrun") a video game. These bugs, along with [cheat codes](/wiki/Cheat_codes "Cheat codes"), [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg_(media) "Easter egg (media)"), and other hidden secrets that were intentionally added to the game can also be exploited.[[30]](#cite_note-WashPost-32)[[31]](#cite_note-KonamiCode-33)[[32]](#cite_note-Wolf-34)[[33]](#cite_note-Patterns-35) On some consoles, [cheat cartridges](/wiki/Cheat_cartridge "Cheat cartridge") allow players to execute these cheat codes, and user-developed [trainers](/wiki/Trainer_(games) "Trainer (games)") allow similar bypassing for computer software games. Both of which might make the game easier, give the player additional power-ups, or change the appearance of the game.[[31]](#cite_note-KonamiCode-33)

## Components

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Arcade_Sugoi_Malmi_Helsinki.jpg/250px-Arcade_Sugoi_Malmi_Helsinki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arcade_Sugoi_Malmi_Helsinki.jpg)

[Arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game") machines at the Sugoi arcade game hall in [Malmi, Helsinki](/wiki/Malmi,_Helsinki "Malmi, Helsinki"), Finland

To distinguish from electronic games, a video game is generally considered to require a platform, the hardware which contains computing elements, to process player interaction from some type of input device and displays the results to a video output display.[[34]](#cite_note-eu_game_industry-36)

Video games require a platform, a specific combination of [electronic components](/wiki/Electronic_component "Electronic component") or [computer hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware") and associated [software](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system"), to operate.[[35]](#cite_note-platformdef-37) The term system is also commonly used. These platforms may include multiple brandsheld by [platform holders](/wiki/Platform_holder "Platform holder"), such as Nintendo or Sony, seeking to gain larger market shares.[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42) Games are typically designed to be played on one or a limited number of platforms, and exclusivity to a platform or brand is used by platform holders as a competitive edge in the video game market.[[41]](#cite_note-43) However, games may be developed for alternative platforms than intended, which are described as [ports](/wiki/Port_(video_gaming) "Port (video gaming)") or conversions. These also may be remasters - where most of the original game's source code is reused and art assets, models, and game levels are updated for modern systems – and remakes, where in addition to asset improvements, significant reworking of the original game and possibly from scratch is performed.[[42]](#cite_note-port_vs_conversion-44)

The list below is not exhaustive and excludes other electronic devices capable of playing video games such as [PDAs](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant") and [graphing calculators](/wiki/Graphing_calculator "Graphing calculator").

PC games
:   [PC games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") involve a player interacting with a [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") (PC) connected to a [video monitor](/wiki/Video_monitor "Video monitor").[[43]](#cite_note-45) Personal computers are not dedicated game platforms, so there may be differences running the same game on different hardware. Also, the openness allows some features to developers like reduced software cost,[[44]](#cite_note-46) increased flexibility, increased innovation, emulation, creation of modifications or [mods](/wiki/Mod_(video_gaming) "Mod (video gaming)"), open hosting for [online gaming](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") (in which a person plays a video game with people who are in a different household) and others. A [gaming computer](/wiki/Gaming_computer "Gaming computer") is a PC or laptop intended specifically for gaming, typically using high-performance, high-cost components. In addition to personal computer gaming, there also exist games that work on [mainframe computers](/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer") and other similarly shared systems, with users logging in remotely to use the computer.

Home console
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/PS2-Fat-Console-Set.jpg/250px-PS2-Fat-Console-Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:PS2-Fat-Console-Set.jpg)

    The [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") is the [best-selling video game console](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_game_consoles "List of best-selling game consoles"), with over 155 million units sold.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

A [console game](/wiki/Console_game "Console game") is played on a [home console](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console"), a specialized electronic device that connects to a common [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set") or [composite video monitor](/wiki/Composite_monitor "Composite monitor"). Home consoles are specifically designed to play games using a dedicated hardware environment, giving developers a concrete hardware target for development and assurances of what features will be available, simplifying development compared to PC game development. Usually consoles only run games developed for it, or games from other platform made by the same company, but never games developed by its direct competitor, even if the same game is available on different platforms. It often comes with a specific [game controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller"). Major console platforms include [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox"), [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") and [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo_video_game_consoles "Nintendo video game consoles").

Handheld console
:   A [handheld game console](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console") is a small, self-contained electronic device that is portable and can be held in a user's hands. It features the console, a small screen, speakers and buttons, [joystick](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick") or other [game controllers](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller") in a single unit. Like consoles, handhelds are dedicated platforms, and share almost the same characteristics. Handheld hardware usually is less powerful than PC or console hardware. Some handheld games from the late 1970s and early 1980s could only play one game. In the 1990s and 2000s, a number of handheld games used cartridges, which enabled them to be used to play many different games. The handheld console has waned in the 2010s as mobile device gaming has become a more dominant factor.

Arcade video game
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Light_gun_survival_horror_arcade_game.jpg/250px-Light_gun_survival_horror_arcade_game.jpg)](/wiki/File:Light_gun_survival_horror_arcade_game.jpg)

    A police-themed arcade game in which players use a [light gun](/wiki/Light_gun "Light gun")

An [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game") generally refers to a game played on an even more specialized type of electronic device that is typically designed to play only one game and is encased in a special, large coin-operated [cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet") which has one built-in console, controllers (joystick, buttons, etc.), a [CRT screen](/wiki/CRT_screen "CRT screen"), and audio amplifier and speakers. Arcade games often have brightly painted logos and images relating to the theme of the game. While most arcade games are housed in a vertical cabinet, which the user typically stands in front of to play, some arcade games use a tabletop approach, in which the display screen is housed in a table-style cabinet with a see-through table top. With table-top games, the users typically sit to play. In the 1990s and 2000s, some arcade games offered players a choice of multiple games. In the 1980s, [video arcades](/wiki/Video_arcade "Video arcade") were businesses in which game players could use a number of arcade video games. In the 2010s, there are far fewer video arcades, but some movie theaters and family entertainment centers still have them.

Browser game
:   A [browser game](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") takes advantages of standardizations of technologies for the functionality of [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") across multiple devices providing a [cross-platform](/wiki/Cross-platform "Cross-platform") environment. These games may be identified based on the website that they appear, such as with [Miniclip](/wiki/Miniclip "Miniclip") games. Others are named based on the programming platform used to develop them, such as [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") and [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash") games.

Mobile game
:   With the introduction of [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer") standardized on the [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") operating systems, [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming") has become a significant platform. These games may use unique features of mobile devices that are not necessary present on other platforms, such as [accelerometers](/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer"), [global positioning information](/wiki/GPS "GPS") and camera devices to support [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") gameplay.

Cloud gaming
:   [Cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") requires a minimal hardware device, such as a basic computer, console, laptop, mobile phone or even a dedicated hardware device connected to a display with good Internet connectivity that connects to hardware systems by the cloud gaming provider. The game is computed and rendered on the remote hardware, using a number of predictive methods to reduce the [network latency](/wiki/Network_latency "Network latency") between player input and output on their display device. For example, the [Xbox Cloud Gaming](/wiki/Xbox_Cloud_Gaming "Xbox Cloud Gaming") and [PlayStation Now](/wiki/PlayStation_Now "PlayStation Now") platforms use dedicated custom [server blade](/wiki/Server_blade "Server blade") hardware in [cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") centers.

Virtual reality
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Gamescom_Playstation_VR_Playseat_%2836454815300%29.jpg/250px-Gamescom_Playstation_VR_Playseat_%2836454815300%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gamescom_Playstation_VR_Playseat_(36454815300).jpg)

    Players using the [PlayStation VR](/wiki/PlayStation_VR "PlayStation VR") headsets in 2017

     [Virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") (VR) games generally require players to use a special [head-mounted unit](/wiki/Head-mounted_display "Head-mounted display") that provides [stereoscopic screens](/wiki/Stereoscopy "Stereoscopy") and [motion tracking](/wiki/Positional_tracking "Positional tracking") to immerse a player within virtual environment that responds to their head movements. Some VR systems include control units for the player's hands as to provide a direct way to interact with the virtual world. VR systems generally require a separate computer, console, or other processing device that couples with the head-mounted unit.

Emulation
:   An [emulator](/wiki/Video_game_console_emulator "Video game console emulator") enables games from a console or otherwise different system to be run in a type of [virtual machine](/wiki/Virtual_machine "Virtual machine") on a modern system, simulating the hardware of the original and allows old games to be played. While emulators themselves have been found to be legal in United States case law, the act of obtaining the game software that one does not already own may violate copyrights. However, there are some official releases of emulated software from game manufacturers, such as Nintendo with its [Virtual Console](/wiki/Virtual_Console "Virtual Console") or [Nintendo Switch Online](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_Online "Nintendo Switch Online") offerings.

Backward compatibility
:   [Backward compatibility](/wiki/Backward_compatibility "Backward compatibility") is similar in nature to emulation in that older games can be played on newer platforms, but typically directly though hardware and built-in software within the platform. The [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") popularized the trend by having the capability of playing past generation games from the [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") via inserting the original game media into the newer console, while Nintendo's [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") could play [GameCube](/wiki/GameCube "GameCube") titles as well in the same manner.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50)

### Game media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/NES-Cartridge.jpg/250px-NES-Cartridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:NES-Cartridge.jpg)

An unlabeled game cartridge for the Nintendo Entertainment System

Early arcade games, home consoles, and handheld games were dedicated hardware units with the game's logic built into the electronic componentry of the hardware. Since then, most video game platforms are considered programmable, having means to read and play multiple games distributed on different types of media or formats. Physical formats include [ROM cartridges](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge"), [magnetic storage](/wiki/Magnetic_storage "Magnetic storage") including [magnetic-tape data storage](/wiki/Magnetic-tape_data_storage "Magnetic-tape data storage") and [floppy discs](/wiki/Floppy_disc "Floppy disc"), [optical media](/wiki/Optical_media "Optical media") formats including [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), and [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory") cards. Furthermore [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") over the Internet or other communication methods as well as [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") alleviate the need for any physical media. In some cases, the media serves as the direct read-only memory for the game, or it may be the form of [installation media](/wiki/Installation_(computer_programs) "Installation (computer programs)") that is used to write the main assets to the player's platform's local storage for faster loading periods and later updates.

Games can be extended with new content and [software patches](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)") through either [expansion packs](/wiki/Expansion_pack "Expansion pack") which are typically available as physical media, or as [downloadable content](/wiki/Downloadable_content "Downloadable content") nominally available via digital distribution. These can be offered freely or can be used to [monetize](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization") a game following its initial release. Several games offer players the ability to create [user-generated content](/wiki/User-generated_content "User-generated content") to share with others to play. Other games, mostly those on personal computers, can be extended with [user-created modifications](/wiki/Mod_(video_games) "Mod (video games)") or mods that alter or add onto the game; these often are unofficial and were developed by players from [reverse engineering](/wiki/Reverse_engineering "Reverse engineering") of the game, but other games provide official support for modding the game.[[49]](#cite_note-lombardi-51)

### Input device

Main article: [Game controller](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Nintendo-Super-NES-Controller.jpg/250px-Nintendo-Super-NES-Controller.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nintendo-Super-NES-Controller.jpg)

A North American [Super NES](/wiki/Super_NES "Super NES") game controller from the early 1990s

Video game can use several types of input devices to translate human actions to a game. Most common are the use of game controllers like [gamepads](/wiki/Gamepad "Gamepad") and [joysticks](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick") for most consoles, and as accessories for personal computer systems along keyboard and mouse controls. Common controls on the most recent controllers include face buttons, shoulder triggers, [analog sticks](/wiki/Analog_stick "Analog stick"), and [directional pads ("d-pads")](/wiki/D-pad "D-pad"). Consoles typically include standard controllers which are shipped or bundled with the console itself, while peripheral controllers are available as a separate purchase from the console manufacturer or third-party vendors.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Similar control sets are built into handheld consoles and onto arcade cabinets. Newer technology improvements have incorporated additional technology into the controller or the game platform, such as [touchscreens](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") and [motion detection](/wiki/Motion_detection "Motion detection") sensors that give more options for how the player interacts with the game. Specialized controllers may be used for certain genres of games, including [racing wheels](/wiki/Racing_wheel "Racing wheel"), [light guns](/wiki/Light_gun "Light gun") and [dance pads](/wiki/Dance_pad "Dance pad"). [Digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") and motion detection can capture movements of the player as input into the game, which can, in some cases, effectively eliminate the control, and on other systems such as virtual reality, are used to enhance immersion into the game.

### Display and output

Main article: [Video game graphics](/wiki/Video_game_graphics "Video game graphics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Game-Boy-FL.jpg/250px-Game-Boy-FL.jpg)](/wiki/File:Game-Boy-FL.jpg)

Handheld units, like the Game Boy, include built-in output screens and sound speakers.

By definition, all video games are intended to output graphics to an external video display, such as [cathode ray tube televisions](/w/index.php?title=Cathode_ray_tube_television&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cathode ray tube television (page does not exist)"), newer [liquid-crystal display](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") (LCD) televisions and built-in screens, [projectors](/wiki/Projector "Projector") or [computer monitors](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), depending on the type of platform the game is played on. Features such as [color depth](/wiki/Color_depth "Color depth"), [refresh rate](/wiki/Refresh_rate "Refresh rate"), [frame rate](/wiki/Frame_rate "Frame rate"), and [screen resolution](/wiki/Screen_resolution "Screen resolution") are a combination of the limitations of the game platform and display device and the program efficiency of the game itself. The game's output can range from fixed displays using LED or LCD elements, [text-based games](/wiki/Text-based_game "Text-based game"), [two-dimensional](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") and [three-dimensional](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") graphics, and [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") displays.

The game's graphics are often accompanied by sound produced by internal speakers on the game platform or external speakers attached to the platform, as directed by the game's programming. This often will include sound effects tied to the player's actions to provide audio feedback, as well as background music for the game.

Some platforms support additional feedback mechanics to the player that a game can take advantage of. This is most commonly [haptic technology](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology") built into the game controller, such as causing the controller to shake in the player's hands to simulate a shaking earthquake occurring in game.

## Classifications

Video games are frequently classified by a number of factors related to how one plays them.

### Genre

Main article: [Video game genre](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre")

See also: [Gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay")

*[Dustforce](/wiki/Dustforce "Dustforce")* is representative of the [platform game](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") genre as its gameplay involves jumping between platforms.

A video game, like most other forms of media, may be categorized into [genres](/wiki/Genres "Genres"). However, unlike film or television which use visual or narrative elements, video games are generally categorized into genres based on their gameplay interaction, since this is the primary means which one interacts with a video game.[[51]](#cite_note-Apperley-53)[[52]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-54)[[53]](#cite_note-Explosion-55) The narrative setting does not impact gameplay; a [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") is still a shooter game, regardless of whether it takes place in a fantasy world or in outer space.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdamsRollings200667-56)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarteveld201171-57) An exception is the [horror game](/wiki/Horror_game "Horror game") genre, used for games that are based on narrative elements of [horror fiction](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction"), the [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural"), and [psychological horror](/wiki/Psychological_horror "Psychological horror").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerron200926–45-58)

Genre names are normally self-describing in terms of the type of gameplay, such as [action game](/wiki/Action_game "Action game"), [role playing game](/wiki/Computer_role_playing_game "Computer role playing game"), or [shoot 'em up](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up"), though some genres have derivations from influential works that have defined that genre, such as [roguelikes](/wiki/Roguelike "Roguelike") from *[Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_(video_game) "Rogue (video game)")*,[[57]](#cite_note-59) [Grand Theft Auto clones](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_clone "Grand Theft Auto clone") from *[Grand Theft Auto III](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_III "Grand Theft Auto III")*,[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELecky-Thompson200823-60) and [battle royale games](/wiki/Battle_royale_game "Battle royale game") from the film *[Battle Royale](/wiki/Battle_Royale_(film) "Battle Royale (film)")*.[[59]](#cite_note-61) The names may shift over time as players, developers and the media come up with new terms; for example, [first-person shooters](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") were originally called "Doom clones" based on [the 1993 game](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArsenault2009149–176-62) A hierarchy of game genres exist, with top-level genres like "shooter game" and "action game" that broadly capture the game's main gameplay style, and several subgenres of specific implementation, such as within the shooter game [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") and [third-person shooter](/wiki/Third-person_shooter "Third-person shooter"). Some cross-genre types also exist that fall until multiple top-level genres such as [action-adventure game](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game").

### Mode

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Winter_2004_DreamHack_LAN_Party.jpg/250px-Winter_2004_DreamHack_LAN_Party.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winter_2004_DreamHack_LAN_Party.jpg)

A [LAN party](/wiki/LAN_party "LAN party") at the 2004 [DreamHack](/wiki/DreamHack "DreamHack") with hundreds of players

A video game's mode describes how many players can use the game at the same type. This is primarily distinguished by [single-player video games](/wiki/Single-player_video_game "Single-player video game") and [multiplayer video games](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game"). Within the latter category, multiplayer games can be played in a variety of ways, including locally at the same device, on separate devices connected through a local network such as [LAN parties](/wiki/LAN_party "LAN party"), or online via separate Internet connections. Most multiplayer games are based on competitive gameplay, but many offer [cooperative and team-based options](/wiki/Cooperative_video_game "Cooperative video game") as well as [asymmetric gameplay](/wiki/Asymmetric_gameplay "Asymmetric gameplay"). [Online games](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") use server structures that can also enable [massively multiplayer online games](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_game "Massively multiplayer online game") (MMOs) to support hundreds of players at the same time.

A small number of video games are [zero-player games](/wiki/Zero-player_game "Zero-player game"), in which the player has very limited interaction with the game itself. These are most commonly simulation games where the player may establish a starting state and then let the game proceed on its own, watching the results as a passive observer, such as with many computerized simulations of [Conway's Game of Life](/wiki/Conway%27s_Game_of_Life "Conway's Game of Life").[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### Types

Most video games are intended for entertainment purposes.[[34]](#cite_note-eu_game_industry-36) Different game types include:

Core games
:   Core or hard-core games refer to the typical perception of video games, developed for entertainment purposes. These games typically require a fair amount of time to learn and master, in contrast to casual games, and thus are most appealing to gamers rather than a broader audience. Most of the AAA video game industry is based around the delivery of core games.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeogh2015152–162-64)

Casual games
:   In contrast to core games, [casual games](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game") are designed for ease of accessibility, simple to understand gameplay and quick to grasp rule sets, and aimed at [mass market](/wiki/Mass_market "Mass market") audience. They frequently support the ability to jump in and out of play on demand, such as during commuting or lunch breaks. Numerous browser and mobile games fall into the casual game area, and casual games often are from genres with low intensity game elements such as [match three](/wiki/Match_three "Match three"), [hidden object](/wiki/Hidden_object "Hidden object"), [time management](/wiki/Time_Management_(video_game_genre) "Time Management (video game genre)"), and [puzzle](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") games.[[63]](#cite_note-65) Causal games frequently use [social-network game](/wiki/Social-network_game "Social-network game") mechanics, where players can enlist the help of friends on their social media networks for extra turns or moves each day.[[64]](#cite_note-66) Popular casual games include *[Tetris](/wiki/Tetris "Tetris")* and *[Candy Crush Saga](/wiki/Candy_Crush_Saga "Candy Crush Saga")*. More recent, starting in the late 2010s, are [hyper-casual games](/wiki/Hyper-casual_game "Hyper-casual game") which use even more simplistic rules for short but infinitely replayable games, such as *[Flappy Bird](/wiki/Flappy_Bird "Flappy Bird")*.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

Educational games
:   [Education software](/wiki/Education_software "Education software") has been used in homes and classrooms to help teach children and students, and video games have been similarly adapted for these reasons, all designed to provide a form of interactivity and entertainment tied to game design elements. There are a variety of differences in their designs and how they educate the user. These are broadly split between [edutainment](/wiki/Educational_entertainment "Educational entertainment") games that tend to focus on the entertainment value and rote learning but are unlikely to engage in [critical thinking](/wiki/Critical_thinking "Critical thinking"), and [educational video games](/wiki/Educational_video_game "Educational video game") that are geared towards problem solving through motivation and positive reinforcement while downplaying the entertainment value.[[66]](#cite_note-68) Examples of educational games include *[The Oregon Trail](/wiki/The_Oregon_Trail_(series) "The Oregon Trail (series)")* and the *[Carmen Sandiego](/wiki/Carmen_Sandiego_(video_game_series) "Carmen Sandiego (video game series)")* series. Further, games not initially developed for educational purposes have found their way into the classroom after release, such as that feature [open worlds](/wiki/Open_world "Open world") or [virtual sandboxes](/wiki/Sandbox_game "Sandbox game") like *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*,[[67]](#cite_note-Education-69) or offer critical thinking skills through [puzzle video games](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") like *[SpaceChem](/wiki/SpaceChem "SpaceChem")*.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Serious games
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/AFA_Beech_in_Flight_Simulator.jpg/250px-AFA_Beech_in_Flight_Simulator.jpg)](/wiki/File:AFA_Beech_in_Flight_Simulator.jpg)

    *[Microsoft Flight Simulator](/wiki/Microsoft_Flight_Simulator "Microsoft Flight Simulator")* is an example of a simulation game.

     Further extending from educational games, [serious games](/wiki/Serious_game "Serious game") are those where the entertainment factor may be augmented, overshadowed, or even eliminated by other purposes for the game. Game design is used to reinforce the non-entertainment purpose of the game, such as using video game technology for the game's interactive world, or [gamification](/wiki/Gamification "Gamification") for reinforcement training. Educational games are a form of serious games, but other types of games include [fitness games](/wiki/Exergaming "Exergaming") that incorporate significant physical exercise to help keep the player fit (such as *[Wii Fit](/wiki/Wii_Fit "Wii Fit")*), simulator games that resemble [flight simulators](/wiki/Flight_simulator "Flight simulator") to pilot aircraft (such as *[Microsoft Flight Simulator](/wiki/Microsoft_Flight_Simulator "Microsoft Flight Simulator")*), [advergames](/wiki/Advergame "Advergame") that are built around the advertising of a product (such as *[Pepsiman](/wiki/Pepsiman_(video_game) "Pepsiman (video game)")*), and [newsgames](/wiki/Newsgame "Newsgame") aimed at conveying a specific advocacy message (such as *[NarcoGuerra](/wiki/NarcoGuerra "NarcoGuerra")*).[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72)

Art games
:   Although video games have been considered an art form on their own, games may be developed to try to purposely communicate a story or message, using the medium as a work of art. These [art or arthouse games](/wiki/Art_game "Art game") are designed to generate emotion and empathy from the player by challenging societal norms and offering critique through the interactivity of the video game medium. They may not have any type of win condition and are designed to let the player explore through the game world and scenarios. Most art games are [indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") in nature, designed based on personal experiences or stories through a single developer or small team. Examples of art games include *[Passage](/wiki/Passage_(video_game) "Passage (video game)")*, *[Flower](/wiki/Flower_(video_game) "Flower (video game)")*, and *[That Dragon, Cancer](/wiki/That_Dragon,_Cancer "That Dragon, Cancer")*.[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-holmes2003-74)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGintere2019346–360-75)

### Content rating

Main article: [Video game content rating system](/wiki/Video_game_content_rating_system "Video game content rating system")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/62/ESRBrating.PNG)](/wiki/File:ESRBrating.PNG)

A typical ESRB rating label, listing the rating and specific content descriptors for *[Rabbids Go Home](/wiki/Rabbids_Go_Home "Rabbids Go Home")*

Video games can be subject to national and international [content rating](/wiki/Content_rating "Content rating") requirements. Like with film content ratings, video game ratings typing identify the target age group that the national or regional ratings board believes is appropriate for the player, ranging from all-ages, to a teenager-or-older, to mature, to the infrequent adult-only games. Most content review is based on the level of violence, both in the type of violence and how [graphic](/wiki/Graphic_violence "Graphic violence") it may be represented, and sexual content, but other themes such as drug and alcohol use and gambling that can influence children may also be identified. A primary identifier based on a minimum age is used by nearly all systems, along with additional descriptors to identify specific content that players and parents should be aware of.

The regulations vary from country to country but generally are voluntary systems upheld by vendor practices, with penalty and fines issued by the ratings body on the video game publisher for misuse of the ratings. Among the major content rating systems include:

* [Entertainment Software Rating Board](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Rating_Board "Entertainment Software Rating Board") (ESRB) that oversees games released in the United States. ESRB ratings are voluntary and rated along a E (Everyone), E10+ (Everyone 10 and older), T (Teen), M (Mature), and AO (Adults Only). Attempts to mandate video games ratings in the U.S. subsequently led to the landmark [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") case, *[Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association](/wiki/Brown_v._Entertainment_Merchants_Association "Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association")* in 2011 which ruled video games were a protected form of art, a key victory for the video game industry.[[74]](#cite_note-legal_results-76)
* [Pan European Game Information](/wiki/Pan_European_Game_Information "Pan European Game Information") (PEGI) covering the United Kingdom, most of the European Union and other European countries, replacing previous national-based systems. The PEGI system uses content rated based on minimum recommended ages, which include 3+, 8+, 12+, 16+, and 18+.
* [Australian Classification Board](/wiki/Australian_Classification_Board "Australian Classification Board") (ACB) oversees the ratings of games and other works in Australia, using ratings of G (General), PG (Parental Guidance), M (Mature), MA15+ (Mature Accompanied), R18+ (Restricted), and X (Restricted for pornographic material). ACB can also deny to give a rating to game (RC – Refused Classification). The ACB's ratings are enforceable by law, and importantly, games cannot be imported or purchased digitally in Australia if they have failed to gain a rating or were given the RC rating, leading to a [number of notable banned games](/wiki/List_of_banned_video_games_in_Australia "List of banned video games in Australia").
* [Computer Entertainment Rating Organization](/wiki/Computer_Entertainment_Rating_Organization "Computer Entertainment Rating Organization") (CERO) rates games for Japan. Their ratings include A (all ages), B (12 and older), C (15 and over), D (17 and over), and Z (18 and over).
* [Unterhaltungssoftware Selbstkontrolle](/wiki/Unterhaltungssoftware_Selbstkontrolle "Unterhaltungssoftware Selbstkontrolle") (USK) rates games for Germany. Their ratings include 0, 6, 12, 16, and 18.

Additionally, the major content system provides have worked to create the [International Age Rating Coalition](/wiki/International_Age_Rating_Coalition "International Age Rating Coalition") (IARC), a means to streamline and align the content ratings system between different region, so that a publisher would only need to complete the content ratings review for one provider, and use the IARC transition to affirm the content rating for all other regions.

Certain nations have even more restrictive rules related to political or ideological content. Within Germany, until 2018, the [Unterhaltungssoftware Selbstkontrolle](/wiki/Unterhaltungssoftware_Selbstkontrolle "Unterhaltungssoftware Selbstkontrolle") (*Entertainment Software Self-Regulation*) would refuse to classify, and thus allow sale, of any game depicting [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") imagery, and thus often requiring developers to replace such imagery with fictional ones. This ruling was relaxed in 2018 to allow for such imagery for "social adequacy" purposes that applied to other works of art.[[75]](#cite_note-77) [China's video game segment](/wiki/Video_games_in_China "Video games in China") is mostly isolated from the rest of the world due to the government's censorship, and all games published there must adhere to strict government review, disallowing content such as smearing the image of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"). Foreign games published in China often require modification by developers and publishers to meet these requirements.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

## Development

Main article: [Video game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development")

See also: [Video game industry practices](/wiki/Video_game_industry_practices "Video game industry practices")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Virtual-camera-system.png/250px-Virtual-camera-system.png)](/wiki/File:Virtual-camera-system.png)

Developers use various tools to create video games. Here an editor is fine-tuning the [virtual camera system](/wiki/Virtual_camera_system "Virtual camera system").

Video game development and authorship, much like any other form of entertainment, is frequently a cross-disciplinary field. [Video game developers](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer"), as employees within this industry are commonly referred to, primarily include [programmers](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer") and [graphic designers](/wiki/Graphic_design "Graphic design"). Over the years, this has expanded to include almost every type of skill that one might see prevalent in the creation of any movie or television program, including [sound designers](/wiki/Sound_design "Sound design"), musicians, and other technicians; as well as skills that are specific to video games, such as the [game designer](/wiki/Game_designer "Game designer"). All of these are managed by [producers](/wiki/Game_producer "Game producer").

In the early days of the industry, it was more common for a single person to manage all of the roles needed to create a video game. As platforms have become more complex and powerful in the type of material they can present, larger teams have been needed to generate all of the art, programming, cinematography, and more. This is not to say that the age of the "one-man shop" is gone, as this is still sometimes found in the casual gaming and handheld markets,[[77]](#cite_note-numberofdevs-79) where smaller games are prevalent due to technical limitations such as limited [RAM](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory") or lack of dedicated 3D graphics rendering capabilities on the target platform (e.g., some [PDAs](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant")).[[78]](#cite_note-80)

Video games are [programmed](/wiki/Computer_programming "Computer programming") like any other piece of computer software. Prior to the mid-1970s, arcade and home consoles were programmed by assembling discrete electro-mechanical components on circuit boards, which limited games to relatively simple logic. By 1975, low-cost [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") were available at volume to be used for video game hardware, which allowed game developers to program more detailed games, widening the scope of what was possible.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKent200165-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82) Ongoing improvements in computer hardware technology have expanded what has become possible to create in video games, coupled with convergence of common hardware between console, computer, and arcade platforms to simplify the development process.[[81]](#cite_note-83) Today, game developers have a number of commercial and [open source](/wiki/Open_source "Open source") tools available for use to make games, often which are across multiple platforms to support portability, or may still opt to create their own for more specialized features and direct control of the game. Today, many games are built around a [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine") that handles the bulk of the game's logic, gameplay, and rendering. These engines can be augmented with specialized engines for specific features, such as a [physics engine](/wiki/Physics_engine "Physics engine") that simulates the physics of objects in real-time. A variety of [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware") exists to help developers access other features, such as playback of videos within games, [network-oriented code](/wiki/Netcode "Netcode") for games that communicate via online services, [matchmaking](/wiki/Matchmaking_(video_games) "Matchmaking (video games)") for online games, and similar features. These features can be used from a developer's programming language of choice, or they may opt to also use [game development kits](/wiki/Game_development_kit "Game development kit") that minimize the amount of direct programming they have to do but can also limit the amount of customization they can add into a game. Like all software, video games usually undergo [quality testing](/wiki/Software_testing "Software testing") before release to assure there are no [bugs](/wiki/Software_bug "Software bug") or [glitches](/wiki/Glitch "Glitch") in the product, though frequently developers will release [patches and updates](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)").

With the growth of the size of development teams in the industry, the problem of cost has increased. Development studios need the best talent, while publishers reduce costs to maintain profitability on their investment. Typically, a video game console development team ranges from 5 to 50 people, and some exceed 100. In May 2009, *[Assassin's Creed II](/wiki/Assassin%27s_Creed_II "Assassin's Creed II")* was reported to have a development staff of 450.[[82]](#cite_note-84) The growth of team size combined with greater pressure to get completed projects into the market to begin recouping production costs has led to a greater occurrence of missed deadlines, rushed games, and the release of unfinished products.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

While amateur and hobbyist game programming had existed since the late 1970s with the introduction of home computers, a newer trend since the mid-2000s is [indie game development](/wiki/Indie_game_development "Indie game development"). Indie games are made by small teams outside any direct publisher control, their games being smaller in scope than those from the larger "[AAA](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)")" game studios, and are often experiments in gameplay and art style. Indie game development is aided by the larger availability of digital distribution, including the newer mobile gaming market, and readily-available and low-cost development tools for these platforms.[[84]](#cite_note-indie_dreams-86)

### Game theory and studies

Main article: [Game studies](/wiki/Game_studies "Game studies")

Although departments of computer science have been studying technical aspects of video games for years, theories that examine games as an artistic medium are a relatively recent development. The two most visible schools in this field are ludology and [narratology](/wiki/Narratology "Narratology"). Narrativists approach video games in the context of what [Janet Murray](/wiki/Janet_Murray "Janet Murray") calls "Cyberdrama". That is to say, their major concern is with video games as a storytelling medium, one that arises out of [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction"). Murray puts video games in the context of the [Holodeck](/wiki/Holodeck "Holodeck"), a fictional piece of technology from *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")*, arguing for the video game as a medium in which the player is allowed to become another person, and to act out in another world.[[85]](#cite_note-87) This image of video games received early widespread popular support, and forms the basis of films such as *[Tron](/wiki/Tron "Tron")*, *[eXistenZ](/wiki/EXistenZ "EXistenZ")* and *[The Last Starfighter](/wiki/The_Last_Starfighter "The Last Starfighter")*.

Ludologists break sharply and radically from this idea. They argue that a video game is first and foremost a game, which must be understood in terms of its rules, interface, and the concept of play that it deploys. [Espen Aarseth](/wiki/Espen_Aarseth "Espen Aarseth") argues that, although games certainly have plots, characters, and aspects of traditional narratives, these aspects are incidental to gameplay. For example, Aarseth is critical of the widespread attention that narrativists have given to the heroine of the game *[Tomb Raider](/wiki/Tomb_Raider "Tomb Raider")*, saying that "the dimensions of [Lara Croft](/wiki/Lara_Croft "Lara Croft")'s body, already analyzed to death by [film theorists](/wiki/Film_theory "Film theory"), are irrelevant to me as a player, because a different-looking body would not make me play differently... When I play, I don't even see her body, but see through it and past it."[[86]](#cite_note-88) Ludologists reject traditional theories of art because they claim the artistic and socially relevant qualities of a video game, are primarily determined by the underlying set of rules, demands, and expectations imposed on the player.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

While many games rely on [emergent principles](/wiki/Emergence "Emergence"), video games commonly present simulated story worlds where emergent behavior occurs within the context of the game. The term "emergent narrative" has been used to describe how, in a simulated environment, storyline can be created simply by "what happens to the player."[[87]](#cite_note-89) However, emergent behavior is not limited to sophisticated games. In general, any place where event-driven instructions occur for [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") in a game, emergent behavior will exist. For instance, take a racing game in which cars are programmed to avoid crashing, and they encounter an obstacle in the track: the cars might then maneuver to avoid the obstacle causing the cars behind them to slow or maneuver to accommodate the cars in front of them and the obstacle. The programmer never wrote code to specifically create a traffic jam, yet one now exists in the game.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Intellectual property for video games

Main articles: [Intellectual property protection of video games](/wiki/Intellectual_property_protection_of_video_games "Intellectual property protection of video games") and [Video game clone](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone")

Most commonly, video games are protected by [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright"), though both [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") and [trademarks](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") have been used as well.

Though local copyright regulations vary to the degree of protection, video games qualify as copyrighted visual-audio works, and enjoy cross-country protection under the [Berne Convention](/wiki/Berne_Convention "Berne Convention").[[88]](#cite_note-90) This typically only applies to the underlying code, as well as to the artistic aspects of the game such as its writing, art assets, and music. Gameplay itself is generally not considered copyrightable; in the United States among other countries, video games are considered to fall into the [idea–expression distinction](/wiki/Idea%E2%80%93expression_distinction "Idea–expression distinction") in that it is how the game is presented and expressed to the player that can be copyrighted, but not the underlying principles of the game.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

Because gameplay is normally ineligible for copyright, gameplay ideas in popular games are often replicated and built upon in other games. At times, this repurposing of gameplay can be seen as beneficial and a fundamental part of how the industry has grown by building on the ideas of others.[[90]](#cite_note-nytimes_clone-92)[[91]](#cite_note-ars_clone-93) For example *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")* (1993) and *[Grand Theft Auto III](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_III "Grand Theft Auto III")* (2001) introduced gameplay that created popular new game genres, the [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") and the [*Grand Theft Auto* clone](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_clone "Grand Theft Auto clone"), respectively, in the few years after their release.[[92]](#cite_note-clones-94)[[93]](#cite_note-bbc_gta_clone-95) However, at times and more frequently at the onset of the industry, developers would intentionally create video game clones of successful games and game hardware with few changes, which led to the flooded arcade and dedicated home console market around 1978.[[90]](#cite_note-nytimes_clone-92)[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[91]](#cite_note-ars_clone-93) Cloning is also a major issue with countries that do not have strong intellectual property protection laws, such as [within China](/wiki/Intellectual_property_in_China "Intellectual property in China"). The lax oversight by China's government and the difficulty for foreign companies to take Chinese entities to court had enabled China to support a large [grey market](/wiki/Grey_market "Grey market") of cloned hardware and software systems.[[95]](#cite_note-97) The industry remains challenged to distinguish between creating new games based on refinements of past successful games to create a new type of gameplay, and intentionally creating a clone of a game that may simply swap out art assets.[[96]](#cite_note-98)
