### History

The early history of the video game industry, following the first game hardware releases and through 1983, had little structure. Video games quickly took off during the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") from the late 1970s to early 1980s, but the newfound industry was mainly composed of game developers with little business experience. This led to numerous companies forming simply to create [clones](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") of popular games to try to capitalize on the market.[[97]](#cite_note-down_many_times-99) Due to loss of publishing control and oversaturation of the market, the North American home video game market [crashed in 1983](/wiki/1983_video_game_crash "1983 video game crash"), dropping from revenues of around $3 billion in 1983 to $100 million by 1985. Many of the North American companies created in the prior years closed down. Japan's growing game industry was briefly shocked by this crash but had sufficient longevity to withstand the short-term effects, and [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") helped to revitalize the industry with the release of the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") in North America in 1985.[[97]](#cite_note-down_many_times-99) Along with it, Nintendo established a number of core industrial practices to prevent unlicensed game development and control game distribution on their platform, methods that continue to be used by console manufacturers today.[[97]](#cite_note-down_many_times-99)

The industry remained more conservative following the 1983 crash, forming around the concept of publisher-developer dichotomies, and by the 2000s, leading to the industry centralizing around low-risk, [triple-A](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)") games and studios with large development budgets of at least $10 million or more.[[98]](#cite_note-100) The advent of the Internet brought digital distribution as a viable means to distribute games, and contributed to the growth of more riskier, experimental [independent game development](/wiki/Independent_game_development "Independent game development") as an alternative to triple-A games in the late 2000s and which has continued to grow as a significant portion of the video game industry.[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[84]](#cite_note-indie_dreams-86)

### Industry roles

Video games have a large [network effect](/wiki/Network_effect "Network effect") that draw on many different sectors that tie into the larger video game industry. While video game developers are a significant portion of the industry, other key participants in the market include:[[100]](#cite_note-102)

* [Publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"): Companies generally that oversee bringing the game from the developer to market. This often includes performing the [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing"), public relations, and advertising of the game. Publishers frequently pay the developers ahead of time to make their games and will be involved in critical decisions about the direction of the game's progress, and then pay the developers additional royalties or bonuses based on sales performances. Other smaller, boutique publishers may simply offer to perform the publishing of a game for a small fee and a portion of the sales, and otherwise leave the developer with the creative freedom to proceed. A range of other publisher-developer relationships exist between these points.
* [Distributors](/wiki/Distribution_(marketing) "Distribution (marketing)"): Publishers often are able to produce their own game media and take the role of distributor, but there are also third-party distributors that can mass-produce game media and distribute to retailers. Digital storefronts like [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") and the [iOS App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)") also serve as distributors and retailers in the digital space.
* Retailers: Physical storefronts, which include large online retailers, department and electronic stores, and specialty video game stores, sell games, consoles, and other accessories to consumers. This has also including a trade-in market in certain regions, allowing players to turn in used games for partial refunds or credit towards other games. However, with the uprising of digital marketplaces and [e-commerce revolution](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce"), retailers have been performing worse than in the past.
* Hardware manufacturers: The [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") manufacturers produce console hardware, often through a value chain system that include numerous component suppliers and [contract manufacturer](/wiki/Contract_manufacturer "Contract manufacturer") that assemble the consoles. Further, these console manufacturers typically require a license to develop for their platform and may control the production of some games, such as Nintendo does with the use of game cartridges for its systems. In exchange, the manufacturers may help promote games for their system and may seek console exclusivity for certain games. For games on personal computers, a number of manufacturers are devoted to high-performance "[gaming computer](/wiki/Gaming_computer "Gaming computer")" hardware, particularly in the graphics card area; several of the same companies overlap with component supplies for consoles. A range of third-party manufacturers also exist to provide equipment and gear for consoles post-sale, such as additional controllers for console or carrying cases and gear for handheld devices.
* [Journalism](/wiki/Video_game_journalism "Video game journalism"): While journalism around video games used to be primarily print-based, and focused more on post-release reviews and gameplay strategy, the Internet has brought a more proactive press that use web journalism, covering games in the months prior to release as well as beyond, helping to build excitement for games ahead of release.
* [Influencers](/wiki/Influencers "Influencers"): With the rising importance of [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), video game companies have found that the opinions of influencers using [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") to play through their games has had a significant impact on game sales, and have turned to use influencers alongside traditional journalism as a means to build up attention to their game before release.
* [Esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports"): Esports is a major function of several multiplayer games with numerous professional leagues established since the 2000s, with large viewership numbers, particularly out of southeast Asia since the 2010s.
* Trade and advocacy groups: Trade groups like the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") were established to provide a common voice for the industry in response to governmental and other advocacy concerns. They frequently set up the [major trade events and conventions](/wiki/List_of_gaming_conventions "List of gaming conventions") for the industry such as [E3](/wiki/E3 "E3").
* [Gamers](/wiki/Gamer "Gamer"): Proactive hobbyists who are players and consumers of video games. While their representation in the industry is primarily seen through game sales, many companies follow gamers' comments on social media or on user reviews and engage with them to work to improve their products in addition to other feedback from other parts of the industry. Demographics of the larger player community also impact parts of the market; while once dominated by younger men, the market shifted in the mid-2010s towards women and older players who generally preferred mobile and causal games, leading to further growth in those sectors.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

### Major regional markets

See also: [Video game industry § International practices](/wiki/Video_game_industry#International_practices "Video game industry")

The industry itself grew out from both the United States and Japan in the 1970s and 1980s before having a larger worldwide contribution. Today, the video game industry is predominantly led by major companies in North America (primarily the United States and Canada), Europe, and southeast Asia including Japan, South Korea, and China. Hardware production remains an area dominated by Asian companies either directly involved in hardware design or part of the production process, but digital distribution and indie game development of the late 2000s has allowed game developers to flourish nearly anywhere and diversify the field.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

### Game sales

See also: [List of best-selling video games](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_video_games "List of best-selling video games") and [Golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Videogameretaildisplay.jpg/250px-Videogameretaildisplay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Videogameretaildisplay.jpg)

A retail display in Switzerland with a large selection of games for platforms popular in the early 2000s

According to the market research firm Newzoo, the global video game industry drew estimated revenues of over $159 billion in 2020. Mobile games accounted for the bulk of this, with a 48% share of the market, followed by console games at 28% and personal computer games at 23%.[[1]](#cite_note-vg_industry_size_2020-3)

Sales of different types of games vary widely between countries due to local preferences. Japanese consumers tend to purchase much more [handheld games](/wiki/Handheld_game "Handheld game") than [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game") and especially [PC games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game"), with a strong preference for games catering to local tastes.[[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106) Another key difference is that, though having [declined in the West](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games"), [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") remain an important sector of the [Japanese gaming industry](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Japan "Video gaming in Japan").[[105]](#cite_note-107) [In South Korea](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_South_Korea "Video gaming in South Korea"), computer games are generally preferred over console games, especially [MMORPG](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_role-playing_game "Massively multiplayer online role-playing game") games and [real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") games. Computer games are also popular in China.[[106]](#cite_note-108)

## Effects on society

### Culture

Main articles: [Video game culture](/wiki/Video_game_culture "Video game culture") and [Gamer](/wiki/Gamer "Gamer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/The_Art_of_Video_Games_2012_%286848246182%29.jpg/250px-The_Art_of_Video_Games_2012_%286848246182%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Art_of_Video_Games_2012_(6848246182).jpg)

*[The Art of Video Games](/wiki/The_Art_of_Video_Games "The Art of Video Games")* exhibit at the [Smithsonian American Art Museum](/wiki/Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum "Smithsonian American Art Museum") in 2012

Video game culture is a worldwide [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media") subculture formed around video games and game playing. As computer and video games have increased in popularity over time, they have had a significant influence on popular culture. Video game culture has also evolved over time hand in hand with [internet culture](/wiki/Internet_culture "Internet culture") as well as the increasing popularity of mobile games. Many people who play video games identify as gamers, which can mean anything from someone who enjoys games to someone who is passionate about it. As video games become more social with [multiplayer](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game") and [online capability](/wiki/Online_game "Online game"), gamers find themselves in growing [social networks](/wiki/Social_network "Social network"). Gaming can both be entertainment as well as competition, as a new trend known as [electronic sports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") is becoming more widely accepted. In the 2010s, video games and discussions of video game trends and topics can be seen in [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), politics, television, film and music. The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_video_game_industry "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the video game industry") during 2020–2021 gave further visibility to video games as a pastime to enjoy with friends and family online as a means of [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing").[[107]](#cite_note-109)[[108]](#cite_note-110)

### Art

Main article: [Video games as an art form](/wiki/Video_games_as_an_art_form "Video games as an art form")

Since the mid-2000s there has been debate whether video games qualify as art, primarily as the form's interactivity interfered with the artistic intent of the work and that they are designed for commercial appeal. A significant debate on the matter came after film critic [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") published an essay "Video Games can never be art",[[109]](#cite_note-ebert_gamesart-111) which challenged the industry to prove him and other critics wrong.[[110]](#cite_note-112) The view that video games were an art form was cemented in 2011 when the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") ruled in the landmark case *[Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association](/wiki/Brown_v._Entertainment_Merchants_Association "Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association")* that video games were a protected form of speech with artistic merit.[[111]](#cite_note-113) Since then, video game developers have come to use the form more for artistic expression, including the development of [art games](/wiki/Art_game "Art game"),[[112]](#cite_note-114) and the cultural heritage of video games as works of arts, beyond their technical capabilities, have been part of major museum exhibits, including *[The Art of Video Games](/wiki/The_Art_of_Video_Games "The Art of Video Games")* at the [Smithsonian American Art Museum](/wiki/Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum "Smithsonian American Art Museum") and toured at other museums from 2012 to 2016.

Main articles: [Film adaptation § Video game adaptation](/wiki/Film_adaptation#Video_game_adaptation "Film adaptation"), and [Hollywood and the video game industry](/wiki/Hollywood_and_the_video_game_industry "Hollywood and the video game industry")

Video games will inspire sequels and other video games within the same franchise, but also have influenced works outside of the video game medium. Numerous [television shows](/wiki/List_of_television_series_based_on_video_games "List of television series based on video games") (both animated and live-action), [films](/wiki/List_of_films_based_on_video_games "List of films based on video games"), [comics](/wiki/List_of_comics_based_on_video_games "List of comics based on video games") and [novels](/wiki/List_of_novels_based_on_video_games "List of novels based on video games") have been created based on existing video game franchises. Because video games are an interactive medium there has been trouble in converting them to these passive forms of media, and typically such works have been critically panned or treated as children's media. For example, until 2019, no video game film had ever been received a "Fresh" rating on [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), but the releases of *[Detective Pikachu](/wiki/Detective_Pikachu_(film) "Detective Pikachu (film)")* (2019) and *[Sonic the Hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_(film) "Sonic the Hedgehog (film)")* (2020), both receiving "Fresh" ratings, shows signs of the film industry having found an approach to adapt video games for the large screen.[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116) That said, some early video game-based films have been highly successful at the box office, such as 1995's *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1995_film) "Mortal Kombat (1995 film)")* and 2001's *[Lara Croft: Tomb Raider](/wiki/Lara_Croft:_Tomb_Raider "Lara Croft: Tomb Raider")*.[[115]](#cite_note-understanding_media-117)

More recently since the 2000s, there has also become a larger appreciation of [video game music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music"), which ranges from [chiptunes](/wiki/Chiptunes "Chiptunes") composed for limited sound-output devices on early computers and consoles, to fully-scored compositions for most modern games. Such music has frequently served as a platform for covers and remixes, and concerts featuring video game soundtracks performed by bands or orchestras, such as *[Video Games Live](/wiki/Video_Games_Live "Video Games Live")*, have also become popular.[[115]](#cite_note-understanding_media-117) Video games also frequently incorporate licensed music, particularly in the area of [rhythm games](/wiki/Rhythm_game "Rhythm game"), furthering the depth of which video games and music can work together.[[115]](#cite_note-understanding_media-117)

Further, video games can serve as a virtual environment under full control of a producer to create new works. With the capability to render 3D actors and settings in real-time, a new type of work [machinima](/wiki/Machinima "Machinima") (short for "machine cinema") grew out from using video game engines to craft narratives.[[116]](#cite_note-118) As video game engines gain higher fidelity, they have also become part of the tools used in more traditional filmmaking. [Unreal Engine](/wiki/Unreal_Engine "Unreal Engine") has been used as a backbone by [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic") for their [StageCraft](/wiki/StageCraft "StageCraft") technology for shows like *[The Mandalorian](/wiki/The_Mandalorian "The Mandalorian")*.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

Separately, video games are also frequently used as part of the promotion and marketing for other media, such as for [films](/wiki/List_of_video_games_based_on_films "List of video games based on films"), [anime](/wiki/List_of_video_games_based_on_anime_or_manga "List of video games based on anime or manga"), and [comics](/wiki/List_of_video_games_based_on_comics "List of video games based on comics"). However, these licensed games in the 1990s and 2000s often had a reputation for poor quality, developed without any input from the intellectual property rights owners, and several of them are considered among [lists of games with notably negative reception](/wiki/List_of_video_games_notable_for_negative_reception "List of video games notable for negative reception"), such as *[Superman 64](/wiki/Superman_64 "Superman 64")*. More recently, with these licensed games being developed by triple-A studios or through studios directly connected to the licensed property owner, there has been a significant improvement in the quality of these games, with an early trendsetting example of *[Batman: Arkham Asylum](/wiki/Batman:_Arkham_Asylum "Batman: Arkham Asylum")*.[[118]](#cite_note-120)

Main articles: [Video games in education](/wiki/Video_games_in_education "Video games in education") and [Video game behavioral effects](/wiki/Video_game_behavioral_effects "Video game behavioral effects")

Besides their entertainment value, appropriately-designed video games have been seen to provide value in education across several ages and comprehension levels. Learning principles found in video games have been identified as possible techniques with which to reform the U.S. education system.[[119]](#cite_note-121) It has been noticed that gamers adopt an attitude while playing that is of such high concentration, they do not realize they are learning, and that if the same attitude could be adopted at school, education would enjoy significant benefits.[[120]](#cite_note-122)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Video_game#Dubious "Talk:Video game")*] Students are found to be "learning by doing" while playing video games while fostering creative thinking.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

Video games are also believed to be beneficial to the mind and body. It has been shown that action video game players have better [hand–eye coordination](/wiki/Hand%E2%80%93eye_coordination "Hand–eye coordination") and [visuo-motor skills](/wiki/Motor_coordination "Motor coordination"), such as their resistance to [distraction](/wiki/Distraction "Distraction"), their sensitivity to information in the [peripheral vision](/wiki/Peripheral_vision "Peripheral vision") and their ability to count briefly presented objects, than nonplayers.[[122]](#cite_note-124) Researchers found that such enhanced abilities could be acquired by training with action games, involving challenges that switch attention between different locations, but not with games requiring concentration on single objects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A 2018 systematic review found evidence that video gaming training had positive effects on cognitive and emotional skills in the adult population, especially with young adults.[[123]](#cite_note-125) A 2019 systematic review also added support for the claim that video games are beneficial to the brain, although the beneficial effects of video gaming on the brain differed by video games types.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

How can video games boost your mental health? – Max Birk ([Eindhoven University of Technology](/wiki/Eindhoven_University_of_Technology "Eindhoven University of Technology"))

Organisers of video gaming events, such as the organisers of the *D-Lux* video game festival in [Dumfries](/wiki/Dumfries "Dumfries"), Scotland, have emphasised the positive aspects video games can have on [mental health](/wiki/Mental_health "Mental health"). Organisers, mental health workers and mental health nurses at the event emphasised the relationships and friendships that can be built around video games and how playing games can help people learn about others as a precursor to discussing the person's mental health.[[125]](#cite_note-127) A study in 2020 from [Oxford University](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University") also suggested that playing video games can be a benefit to a person's mental health. The report of 3,274 gamers, all over the age of 18, focused on the games [Animal Crossing: New Horizons](/wiki/Animal_Crossing:_New_Horizons "Animal Crossing: New Horizons") and [Plants vs Zombies: Battle for Neighborville](/wiki/Plants_vs_Zombies:_Battle_for_Neighborville "Plants vs Zombies: Battle for Neighborville") and used actual play-time data. The report found that those that played more games tended to report greater "wellbeing".[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129) Also in 2020, [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") professor [Regan Mandryk](/wiki/Regan_Mandryk "Regan Mandryk") of the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan") said her research also showed that video games can have health benefits such as reducing [stress](/wiki/Psychological_stress "Psychological stress") and improving mental health. The university's research studied all age groups – "from pre-literate children through to older adults living in long term care homes" – with a main focus on 18 to 55-year-olds.[[128]](#cite_note-130)

A study of gamers attitudes towards gaming which was reported about in 2018 found that [millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials") use video games as a key strategy for coping with stress. In the study of 1,000 gamers, 55% said that it "helps them to unwind and relieve stress ... and half said they see the value in gaming as a method of escapism to help them deal with daily work pressures".[[129]](#cite_note-131)

Main article: [Video game controversies](/wiki/Video_game_controversies "Video game controversies")

See also: [Digital media use and mental health § Autism](/wiki/Digital_media_use_and_mental_health#Autism "Digital media use and mental health")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Compulsion_loop_for_video_games.svg/250px-Compulsion_loop_for_video_games.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Compulsion_loop_for_video_games.svg)

The [compulsion loop](/wiki/Compulsion_loop "Compulsion loop") for video games is believed to trigger [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") release that can encourage [addictive behavior](/wiki/Addictive_behavior "Addictive behavior").

Video games have caused controversy since the 1970s.[[130]](#cite_note-132) Parents and children's advocates regularly raise concerns that [violent video games](/wiki/Violence_and_video_games "Violence and video games") can influence young players into performing those violent acts in real life, and events such as the [Columbine High School massacre](/wiki/Columbine_High_School_massacre "Columbine High School massacre") in 1999 in which some claimed the perpetrators specifically alluded to using video games to plot out their attack, raised further fears.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Medical experts and mental health professionals have also raised concerned that [video games may be addictive](/wiki/Video_game_addiction "Video game addiction"), and the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") has included "gaming disorder" in the 11th revision of its [International Statistical Classification of Diseases](/wiki/International_Statistical_Classification_of_Diseases "International Statistical Classification of Diseases"). Other health experts, including the [American Psychiatric Association](/wiki/American_Psychiatric_Association "American Psychiatric Association"), have stated that there is insufficient evidence that video games can create violent tendencies or lead to addictive behavior,[[131]](#cite_note-nytimes_20190805-133) though agree that video games typically use a [compulsion loop](/wiki/Compulsion_loop "Compulsion loop") in their core design that can create [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") that can help reinforce the desire to continue to play through that compulsion loop and potentially lead into violent or addictive behavior.[[132]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-gamasutra_kim-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136) Even with case law establishing that video games qualify as a protected art form, there has been pressure on the video game industry to keep their products in check to avoid over-excessive violence particularly for games aimed at younger children. The potential addictive behavior around games, coupled with increased used of post-sale monetization of video games, has also raised concern among parents, advocates, and government officials about [gambling](/wiki/Gambling "Gambling") tendencies that may come from video games, such as controversy around the use of [loot boxes](/wiki/Loot_box "Loot box") in many high-profile games.

Numerous other controversies around video games and its industry have arisen over the years, among the more notable incidents include the [1993 United States Congressional hearings](/wiki/1993_congressional_hearings_on_video_games "1993 congressional hearings on video games") on violent games like *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat")* which led to the formation of the ESRB ratings system, numerous legal actions taken by attorney [Jack Thompson](/wiki/Jack_Thompson_(activist) "Jack Thompson (activist)") over violent games such as *[Grand Theft Auto III](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_III "Grand Theft Auto III")* and *[Manhunt](/wiki/Manhunt_(video_game) "Manhunt (video game)")* from 2003 to 2007, the outrage over the "[No Russian](/wiki/No_Russian "No Russian")" level from *[Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2](/wiki/Call_of_Duty:_Modern_Warfare_2 "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2")* in 2009 which allowed the player to shoot a number of innocent non-player characters at an airport, and the [Gamergate harassment campaign](/wiki/Gamergate_(harassment_campaign) "Gamergate (harassment campaign)") in 2014 that highlighted misogyny from a portion of the player demographic. The industry as a whole has also dealt with issues related to gender, racial, and [LGBTQ+](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") discrimination and mischaracterization of these minority groups in video games. A further issue in the industry is related to working conditions, as development studios and publishers frequently use "[crunch time](/wiki/Crunch_time_(video_gaming) "Crunch time (video gaming)")", required extended working hours, in the weeks and months ahead of a game's release to assure on-time delivery.

## Collecting and preservation

Main articles: [Video game collecting](/wiki/Video_game_collecting "Video game collecting") and [Video game preservation](/wiki/Video_game_preservation "Video game preservation")

See also: [List of video game museums](/wiki/List_of_video_game_museums "List of video game museums")

Players of video games often maintain collections of games. More recently there has been interest in [retro gaming](/wiki/Retro_gaming "Retro gaming"), focusing on games from the first decades. Games in retail packaging in good shape have become collector's items for the early days of the industry, with some rare publications having gone for over US$100,000 as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game&action=edit). Separately, there is also concern about the preservation of video games, as both game media and the hardware to play them degrade over time. Further, many of the game developers and publishers from the first decades no longer exist, so records of their games have disappeared. Archivists and preservations have worked within the scope of copyright law to save these games as part of the cultural history of the industry.

There are many video game museums around the world, including the [National Videogame Museum](/wiki/National_Videogame_Museum_(United_States) "National Videogame Museum (United States)") in [Frisco, Texas](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas"),[[135]](#cite_note-137) which serves as the largest museum wholly dedicated to the display and preservation of the industry's most important artifacts.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Europe hosts video game museums such as the [Computer Games Museum](/wiki/Computerspielemuseum_Berlin "Computerspielemuseum Berlin") in Berlin[[137]](#cite_note-139) and the [Museum of Soviet Arcade Machines](/wiki/Museum_of_Soviet_Arcade_Machines "Museum of Soviet Arcade Machines") in Moscow and Saint-Petersburg.[[138]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) The [Museum of Art and Digital Entertainment](/wiki/Museum_of_Art_and_Digital_Entertainment "Museum of Art and Digital Entertainment") in [Oakland, California](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California") is a dedicated video game museum focusing on playable exhibits of console and computer games.[[140]](#cite_note-142) The [Video Game Museum of Rome](/wiki/Video_Game_Museum_of_Rome "Video Game Museum of Rome") is also dedicated to preserving video games and their history.[[141]](#cite_note-143) The [International Center for the History of Electronic Games](/wiki/International_Center_for_the_History_of_Electronic_Games "International Center for the History of Electronic Games") at [The Strong](/wiki/The_Strong "The Strong") in [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") contains one of the largest collections of electronic games and game-related historical materials in the world, including a 5,000-square-foot (460 m2) exhibit which allows guests to play their way through the history of video games.[[142]](#cite_note-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146) The [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") in Washington, DC has three video games on permanent display: *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")*, *[Dragon's Lair](/wiki/Dragon%27s_Lair_(1983_video_game) "Dragon's Lair (1983 video game)")*, and *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

The [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") has added a total of [20 video games and one video game console](/wiki/List_of_video_games_in_the_Museum_of_Modern_Art "List of video games in the Museum of Modern Art") to its permanent Architecture and Design Collection since 2012.[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149) In 2012, the [Smithsonian American Art Museum](/wiki/Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum "Smithsonian American Art Museum") ran an exhibition on "[The Art of Video Games](/wiki/The_Art_of_Video_Games "The Art of Video Games")".[[148]](#cite_note-150) However, the reviews of the exhibit were mixed, including questioning whether video games belong in an art museum.[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152)
